title: South Asia
subtitle: Stupas, cave temples, bronze gods, and marble mausoleums.
intro: Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, and Islamic traditions layered on top of each other for two millennia. Much of the greatest work is architecture carved straight out of living rock.

works.title: Mohenjo-daro
works.meta: Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2500 BCE · Pakistan
works.desc: A Bronze Age city with grid streets, covered drains, and a Great Bath. Its script is still undeciphered.

works.title: Great Stupa at Sanchi
works.desc: A hemispherical mound containing relics of the Buddha. The four carved gateways around it narrate his life without ever depicting him directly.

works.title: Gandhara Buddha
works.desc: The first sculptural images of the Buddha, made in schist and draped in togas. A crossroads style born from Alexander's successors.

works.title: Ajanta Caves
works.meta: Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE – 6th c. CE · Maharashtra
works.desc: Thirty rock-cut monasteries with surviving murals of the Buddha's past lives. Abandoned, forgotten, and rediscovered by a tiger-hunting British officer in 1819.

works.title: Nataraja (Shiva as Lord of Dance)
works.desc: Shiva dances the universe into being and out of it again, surrounded by a ring of flame, trampling the demon of ignorance.

works.title: Brihadeeswarar Temple
works.desc: A granite tower 216 feet high, capped with a single 80-ton stone. A thousand years old and still in daily ritual use.

works.title: Khajuraho Temples
works.desc: Hindu and Jain temples covered in dense sculpture — gods, battles, and the frank erotic scenes the site is best known for.

works.title: Konark Sun Temple
works.desc: A massive stone chariot for the sun god, pulled by seven horses, wheels carved to tell the time from their shadows.

works.title: Hampi (Vijayanagara)
works.desc: Capital of the last great Hindu empire in the south. Sacked in 1565; the boulder-strewn ruins sprawl over 16 square miles.

works.title: Meenakshi Temple
works.desc: Fourteen gateway towers covered in thousands of painted figures. The whole complex is repainted every twelve years.

works.title: Taj Mahal
works.desc: A tomb Shah Jahan built for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. The marble is inlaid with 28 kinds of gem and stone.

works.title: Sigiriya
works.desc: A palace on top of a 660-foot granite plug. Its western face once carried 500 paintings of celestial women; twenty-one still survive.

works.title: Bhimbetka Rock Shelters
works.desc: Painted caves with some of the oldest hunting scenes on earth — a human riding a gigantic red animal, drawn in ochre and charcoal.

works.title: Fatehpur Sikri
works.desc: A red sandstone capital built and abandoned in fifteen years when the water ran out. The ghost city of the greatest of the Mughals.

works.title: Dilwara Temples
works.desc: White marble carved so finely the columns look more like lace than stone. Every surface is covered — floor, ceiling, arch.

works.title: Elephanta Caves
works.desc: Rock-cut Shiva temple on an island. The Trimurti — Shiva as creator, preserver, and destroyer — emerges twenty feet tall from the living stone.

works.title: Shore Temple of Mahabalipuram
works.desc: One of the earliest stone temples in South India. It stands on the Bay of Bengal; the 2004 tsunami briefly receded far enough to reveal more submerged temples.

works.title: Pattadakal
works.desc: A proving ground for Indian temple architecture — northern and southern styles built side by side for direct comparison before being exported across the subcontinent.

works.title: Ranakpur Jain Temple
works.desc: 1,444 carved marble columns, no two alike. The complex is aligned so that the sun reaches the central sanctum for only a few days a year.

works.title: Humayun's Tomb
works.desc: The first garden tomb of the Mughals and the prototype for the Taj Mahal eighty years later. A red sandstone dome in a four-quartered paradise garden.

works.title: Golden Temple
works.desc: The holiest site of Sikhism. Marble walls inlaid with gemstone, upper floors covered in gold leaf, the whole thing floating on a sacred pool.

works.title: Hawa Mahal
works.desc: The "Palace of Winds" — a honeycomb facade of 953 latticed windows, built so the women of the court could watch the street without being seen.

works.title: Amber Fort
works.desc: Hilltop fort-palace of the Kachhwaha dynasty. The Hall of Mirrors inside glitters with thousands of convex glass inlays; a single candle lights the whole room.

works.title: Jantar Mantar
works.desc: An observatory of huge masonry instruments — sundials ninety feet tall, instruments to locate any star. Built to correct the drifting calendars of Mughal India.

works.title: Victoria Memorial
works.desc: A white marble domed memorial to Queen Victoria, built at the height of imperial self-regard. Now a museum of the Raj and the nationalism that ended it.

works.title: Mughal Miniature Painting
works.desc: Album leaves commissioned by the Mughal emperors — historical chronicles, portraits, and natural history drawn with jeweller's precision.

works.title: Lion Capital of Ashoka
works.desc: Four back-to-back lions atop a polished sandstone column, once topped by a wheel. Adopted as the national emblem of India in 1950.

works.title: Gal Vihara
works.desc: Four colossal Buddhas carved from a single granite wall — seated, standing, reclining, meditating. The face of the reclining Buddha is nine meters long.

works.title: Gol Gumbaz
works.desc: Tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah. The dome is the second-largest in the world, and the whispering gallery underneath it repeats a voice seven times.

works.title: Red Fort
works.desc: Red sandstone walls two kilometers around. Inside, the Diwan-i-Khas held the Peacock Throne until Nadir Shah of Persia took it to Isfahan in 1739.

works.title: Jama Masjid, Delhi
works.desc: The largest mosque in India — a red sandstone and marble courtyard for 25,000. Built in the same decade as the Taj Mahal, by the same emperor.

works.title: Pancha Rathas
works.desc: Five monolithic shrines carved from a single granite ridge in the shape of temple chariots. Each is a different architectural experiment — the prototypes for a millennium of South Indian temples.

works.title: Nalanda Mahavihara
works.desc: A Buddhist university that drew ten thousand monks from across Asia. Burned by Bakhtiyar Khalji around 1193; the library, they say, smoked for months.

works.title: Mahabodhi Temple
works.desc: Built at the spot the Buddha attained enlightenment under a Bodhi tree. A direct descendant of that tree still grows beside the temple.

works.title: Akbar's Tomb
works.desc: A five-story mausoleum on a platform in a paradise garden. Akbar designed much of it himself; Jahangir's later changes produced the strange tiered result.

works.title: Amaravati Stupa Reliefs
works.meta: Satavahana · 2nd c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Andhra Pradesh
works.desc: A Buddhist stupa whose limestone casing was carved with some of the earliest Buddha figures in India. Most pieces are now in the British Museum and the Chennai Government Museum.

works.title: Karla Caves
works.desc: The largest rock-cut chaitya hall in India — a barrel-vaulted cave church with a lantern roof carved in stone to mimic wood beams it never had.

works.title: Mathura Bodhisattva
works.desc: Red sandstone figures in a distinctly Indian idiom — broad-shouldered, loose-robed, flatter and more frontal than the Greco-Buddhist Gandhara school evolving to the northwest.

works.title: Rampurva Bull Capital
works.desc: An Ashokan pillar capital: a single polished Chunar sandstone bull, so highly burnished it was mistaken for metal. One of the finest survivals of the Mauryan court style.

works.title: Bhaja Caves
works.desc: Twenty-two rock-cut viharas and a chaitya, with wooden ribs imitated in stone. Reliefs show figures riding elephants, dancing — a rare look at secular imagery in early Buddhist art.

works.title: Jagannath Temple, Puri
works.desc: Home of the annual Rath Yatra — giant wooden chariots hauled through the streets carrying the three deities. The temple's cooks feed the god daily from one of the largest kitchens in the world.

works.title: Lepakshi Temple
works.desc: A granite temple to Shiva whose ceiling holds the largest surviving Vijayanagara mural. One of its columns hangs a fraction of an inch above the floor — pilgrims pass cloth beneath it.

works.title: Thanjavur Painting
works.desc: Devotional paintings built up in gesso, then layered in gold leaf, semi-precious stones, and mirrored glass. Surface so encrusted the figures seem to float off the board.

works.title: Harappa
works.meta: Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2600–1900 BCE · Pakistan
works.desc: One of the two great Indus cities, grid-planned like Mohenjo-daro. Its granary, citadel, and burial mounds lie under centuries of British railway ballast quarried from the ruins.

works.title: Priest-King of Mohenjo-daro
works.desc: A small bearded steatite bust, head-band and trefoil-patterned robe. Its title is a guess: we don't know if Mohenjo-daro had priests, kings, or this man at all.

works.title: Pashupati Seal
works.desc: A steatite seal of a seated, horned figure surrounded by animals — sometimes read as a proto-Shiva, sometimes a shaman. We can't read the script that runs along the top.

works.title: Buddhas of Bamiyan
works.desc: Two standing Buddhas, 38 and 55 meters tall, carved into a sandstone cliff on the Silk Road. The Taliban dynamited them in 2001; projection mapping now fills the empty niches on quiet nights.

works.title: Dhamek Stupa
works.desc: Solid brick stupa marking the spot of the Buddha's first sermon. Its upper band of geometric floral carving is some of the finest Gupta ornament anywhere.

works.title: Modhera Sun Temple
works.desc: Temple to Surya oriented so the first rays of the equinox sun strike the inner sanctum. A cistern called Ramakunda in front holds over a hundred miniature shrines along its steps.

works.title: Chand Baori
works.desc: A thirteen-story stepwell carved with 3,500 narrow steps into dizzying geometric patterns down its walls. One of the oldest and deepest stepwells in India.

works.title: Somnath Temple
works.desc: A Hindu pilgrimage site destroyed and rebuilt seven times — plundered most famously by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1025. The current temple was built after Indian independence, at the sea's edge.

works.title: Rani ki Vav
works.desc: A subterranean stepwell dug by Queen Udayamati for her husband. Seven levels down, 500 sculptures of Vishnu avatars and apsaras. Silted over by the Saraswati; rediscovered in the 1980s.

works.title: Mysore Palace
works.desc: The current incarnation — fourth to stand on the site — is a wedding cake of Hindu, Mughal, Rajput, and Gothic elements. Lit up nightly by nearly a hundred thousand bulbs.

works.title: Mehrangarh Fort
works.desc: A hundred-meter cliff-top fort in the Blue City of Jodhpur. Its walls still bear handprints of the widows of maharajas who threw themselves on the pyre in the medieval practice of sati.

works.title: Hoysaleswara Temple
works.desc: Carved from chloritic schist, which hardens after quarrying, enabling the Hoysala sculptors to cut almost impossibly thin details. Elephants, musicians, and gods wrap the plinth in horizontal registers that add up to miles of relief never repeating itself.

works.title: Chennakeshava Temple, Somanathapura
works.desc: A star-plan temple with three shrines on a single platform, surrounded by a cloistered courtyard. Every surface is carved: gods in scalloped niches, musicians in frothing bands, a frieze of marching elephants at ground level supporting the whole weight of the building.

works.title: Kailasa Temple, Ellora
works.desc: The largest monolithic sculpture on earth — a full Dravida-style temple carved top-down from a single basalt cliff, freeing perhaps two hundred thousand tonnes of rock. Krishna I's masons worked to a plan no draft of which survives; many believe none existed.

works.title: Virupaksha Temple, Hampi
works.desc: The only temple in Hampi still in continuous worship after the sack of 1565. Its nine-tiered gopura pours down layers of painted deities; in the courtyard a pinhole in the outer wall projects the gopura inverted onto the opposite wall — a Vijayanagara camera obscura.

works.title: Airavatesvara Temple
works.desc: Rajaraja II's jewel-box Shiva temple, its front mandapa shaped like a chariot pulled by stone horses. The plinth is carved with musicians, dancers, and — charmingly — optical-illusion animals: a bull that doubles as an elephant depending on which end you start from.

works.title: Gangaikonda Cholapuram
works.desc: Rajendra Chola built this temple to commemorate his invasion of the Gangetic plain, bringing back a pot of Ganges water poured into the sanctum. The vimana is a softer, more feminine echo of his father's Brihadeeswarar at Thanjavur.

works.title: Chola Bronzes
works.desc: Hollow-cast bronze icons of Shiva, Vishnu, and their consorts, produced by the lost-wax method for temple processions. The dynasty's patronage pushed Indian metal sculpture to its aesthetic high point — even today casters in Swamimalai work to thousand-year-old Chola templates.

works.title: Dashavatara Temple, Deogarh
works.desc: One of the earliest surviving Hindu stone temples, a compact cube topped by a now-damaged tower. Its relief panels — Vishnu asleep on Ananta, Nara-Narayana, the rescue of the elephant king Gajendra — are foundational images of Hindu narrative art.

works.title: Gommateshwara Statue at Shravanabelagola
works.desc: A 17-meter monolithic nude of the Jain ascetic Bahubali, absorbed in meditation so deep that vines have grown up his legs. Every twelve years, pilgrims anoint the figure with milk, turmeric, and sandalwood in the Mahamastakabhisheka festival.

works.title: Paro Taktsang (Tiger's Nest Monastery)
works.desc: Perched on a cliff 900 meters above the Paro valley, on the ledge where Guru Rinpoche was said to have meditated for three years, three months, three weeks, three days, and three hours. A 1998 fire destroyed most of it; the rebuild faithfully repeats the original timber joinery.

works.title: Punakha Dzong
works.desc: Bhutan's second dzong, built at the confluence of two rivers and still the winter home of the monastic body. Its five-story central utse leans out over the water; inside, eighteenth-century murals of the Buddha's life glow in whale-oil lamplight.

works.title: Boudhanath Stupa
works.desc: One of the largest stupas in Asia, thirty-six meters across, its dome topped by the painted eyes of the Buddha watching in four directions. Circumambulated daily by Tibetan refugees, pilgrims, and locals — prayer-wheel grooves are worn smooth into the granite.

works.title: Swayambhunath
works.desc: The 'monkey temple' atop a hill in the Kathmandu Valley, reached by 365 steps flanked by polychrome Buddhas. A 2015 earthquake split several subsidiary shrines; the main stupa, its painted eyes impassive, rode out the shaking.

works.title: Patan Durbar Square
works.desc: The royal plaza of the Malla kings of Patan, edged with Newar pagodas and a stone Krishna Mandir carved in imitation of north Indian shikharas. Several temples collapsed in 2015 and have been reconstructed with timber dowels rather than steel.

works.title: Jokhang Temple
works.desc: Tibet's holiest temple, founded by King Songtsen Gampo to house the Jowo Shakyamuni statue brought by his Tang Chinese wife. Pilgrims complete three prostrations every few steps around the Barkhor circuit outside; the flagstones are polished to a mirror.

works.title: Samye Monastery
works.desc: The first monastery built in Tibet, laid out as a three-dimensional mandala with the central temple representing Mount Meru and four chapels for the cosmic continents. Its founder Trisong Detsen imported Indian masters to ordain the first seven Tibetan monks.

works.title: Thangka Painting
works.meta: Tibetan Buddhist · c. 11th century onward · Himalayan region
works.desc: Scroll paintings on cotton or silk, bordered with brocade, depicting deities in complex mandalic geometries for visualization practice. Pigments are ground from powdered turquoise, cinnabar, and malachite — gold highlights are burnished with a dog's tooth.
